title: A Real-Time Command Center for Mining Operations
excerpt: High-scale telemetry, live monitoring, and optimisation for industrial mining — turning sensor noise into operational decisions.

seo.title: Case Study: Mining Operations Command Center | Baalvion
seo.description: How Baalvion built a real-time command center ingesting high-scale mining telemetry into live dashboards and optimisation on the Baalvion Operating System.

customFields.challenge: Sensor data scattered across sites with no live, unified operational picture.
customFields.solution: A streaming telemetry pipeline feeding a real-time command center with alerting and optimisation.
customFields.architecture: High-throughput ingestion, time-series storage, stream processing, threshold and anomaly alerting.
lessons: Model the data contract before the dashboard.
Alert on trends, not just thresholds.
Operators trust systems that explain themselves.

blocks.text: Each mining site streamed data from hundreds of sensors — equipment health, throughput, environmental readings — into local systems that never spoke to each other. Leadership had no live operational picture, and problems were typically discovered only after they caused downtime. The operator needed a single command center that could turn high-volume sensor noise into timely decisions.

We built a streaming telemetry pipeline on the Baalvion Operating System that ingests sensor data at scale, stores it as time series, and feeds a real-time command center. Operators see live equipment status across every site, with anomaly detection surfacing developing problems before they become outages.

items: **High-throughput ingestion** sized for hundreds of sensors per site.
**Time-series storage** optimised for fast range queries.
**Live dashboards** giving one operational picture across all sites.
**Anomaly and threshold alerting** that flags trends, not just breaches.

blocks.text: Sensors publish to a high-throughput ingestion layer that normalises readings into a strict data contract. A stream processor computes rolling aggregates and runs anomaly detection, writing both raw and derived series to a time-series store. The command center reads from there for live dashboards, and the alerting service notifies operators the moment a trend crosses a learned bound. The approach mirrors [observability for distributed systems](/news/tech/observability-for-distributed-systems).

Stream ingestion, a time-series database, real-time dashboards, anomaly detection, and an alerting service — delivered through our [automation](/services/automation) and [cloud solutions](/services/cloud-solutions) practices for the [manufacturing](/industries/manufacturing) sector.

Telemetry now reaches operators in seconds rather than after the fact. Unplanned downtime dropped by roughly 28% as developing equipment issues were caught early, and every site is visible in a single operational view.

Defining the sensor data contract before building the dashboard prevented a swamp of inconsistent readings. Alerting on trends rather than raw thresholds cut alert fatigue dramatically. And operators only trusted the system once it could explain *why* it raised an alert — explainability mattered as much as accuracy.

faqs.q: How much sensor data can the pipeline handle?
faqs.a: It is sized for hundreds of sensors per site across many sites, ingesting and processing telemetry with second-level latency.

faqs.q: How does anomaly detection avoid false alarms?
faqs.a: It alerts on learned trends and rolling aggregates rather than static thresholds, which sharply reduces noise and alert fatigue.
Yes — every site feeds one command center, giving leadership a single live operational picture across the whole operation.

faqs.q: Which industries does this apply to?
faqs.a: Any operation with high-volume telemetry — see [Baalvion for manufacturing](/industries/manufacturing) and [logistics](/industries/logistics).
